passages.text: Calcaneal spur detection from lateral foot radiographs using deep feature engineering

Deep learning achieves high accuracy in medical imaging but requires large datasets. Performance decreases in small datasets. This study proposes a deep feature engineering (DFE) framework for calcaneal spur detection in X-ray images. A curated dataset of 775 X-ray images was analyzed, with 316 labeled as no finding and 459 as calcaneal spur. The framework has five phases: (i) feature extraction from 19 pretrained CNNs, (ii) feature selection with iterative neighborhood component analysis (INCA), (iii) classification with a t-algorithm-based k-nearest neighbors (tkNN) ensemble, (iv) generation of voted outcomes through iterative majority voting (IMV), and (v) final selection using a greedy algorithm. The framework achieved 93.42% accuracy. Nineteen CNN outcomes and seventeen IMV-based voted outcomes were evaluated. The greedy step selected the result with the highest accuracy. Spur detection reached higher sensitivity than no finding detection, reflecting the visual similarity between normal heels and early spur cases. The proposed DFE framework attains high accuracy on a small biomedical dataset. Its integration of CNN-based features, INCA, tkNN, IMV, and greedy selection provides a lightweight and generalizable method for medical image classification.
Introduction

Heel pain is one of the most common reasons for visits to orthopedic clinics and is often associated with plantar fasciopathy. Plantar fasciopathy is a clinical condition that may include calcaneal spur (enthesophyte) formation and is considered an important etiological factor in heel pain. Although the relationship between calcaneal spurs and symptoms is controversial in the literature, the presence of spurs is often seen in conjunction with thickening of the plantar fascia and is taken into account in clinical evaluations. Calcaneal spur formation is associated primarily with biomechanical factors. These factors include; advanced age, obesity, long periods of standing, repeated micro-trauma, and decreased ankle dorsiflexion. For the evaluation of heel pain using imaging studies the most common study is the lateral view of the foot on plain film. While the primary limitation of this method is that it does not allow direct visualization of findings from plantar fasciitis, it is frequently employed for initial screening purposes. Ultrasonography and MRI are preferred methods when dealing with more chronic or complicated cases, as they provide better resolution for a more thorough assessment of plantar fascia pathology including thickening or edema.

The use of radiographs as an information source for clinical decision support systems has many advantages including; radiographs are relatively inexpensive, readily available, and quickly applied. However, there may be significant variability in how radiologists interpret radiographs, which can complicate making accurate diagnoses especially when early stage spur formation appears similar to typical heel images. Thus, developing CAD (computer-aided diagnosis) systems that are either completely or partially automated would be of value to clinicians by helping them make informed decisions when using radiology. There has been significant advancement in DL (deep learning) methods used for medical image analysis over the last few years and as a result, they have provided accurate results in both classification and lesion detection, but these models are dependent upon having access to large amounts of data and high levels of computing capability to produce successful outcomes. Additionally, it is anticipated that no one DL architecture will provide the best possible outcomes for all types of tasks.

With respect to Deep Feature Engineering (DFE), we are now in a position to apply many of the ideas previously discussed. The use of pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for the extraction of deep features combined with the application of the traditional methods of feature selection and classification offers a lighter, more flexible, and more generally applicable alternative to the aforementioned architectures. Because of its ability to produce good results on small datasets, DFE is particularly well-suited to problems such as the detection of calcaneal spurs from routine lateral foot radiographs. A DFE approach based upon the combination of the features produced by 19 different pre-trained CNNs enhanced by INCA-based feature selection and a tkNN classifier is proposed in this paper. The purpose of this DFE approach is to obtain accurate classifications of calcaneal spur presence/absence, to achieve consistent performance regardless of the amount of training data available, and to facilitate clinical decision making. Thus, it aims to increase consistency in conservative treatment planning and to strengthen clinician-patient communication.
Literature review

Plantar fasciopathy and calcaneal spur are among the most common causes of heel pain and are clinical conditions that require careful evaluation in both diagnosis and treatment. In recent years, artificial intelligence and deep learning-based methods have begun to play an important role in medical image analysis and clinical decision support systems. These developments enable more objective, rapid, and reproducible results, particularly in different modalities such as radiography, ultrasound, and pressure sensor-based images. Taher and Özacar presented HeCapsNet, a capsule network for heel disease detection from lateral foot radiographs. They extended the baseline CapsNet with convolution, dropout and max-pooling layers. Their dataset included 3,956 images. They also utilized YOLOv8 to aid the classification step. Their model obtained a test accuracy of 97.29% on the balanced dataset and 95.77% accuracy under five-fold cross validation. Kim et al. designed a deep learning model for insole prescription based on clinical and biomechanical data of 838 patients with foot pain. The variables that were entered into the model were pelvic obliquity, anterversions and retroversion. Their system forecasted foot posture index and the necessity for supportive components according to modified Root technique. They experimented with two deep neural network regression models, and five multi-class deep neural network classifier models. Their validation accuracy varied from 72% to 95%, and the area under receiver operating characteristic curve was between 0.79 and 0.94. Their models achieved greater than 90% accuracy at both heel lift and full lift decisions. Roop et al. introduced a self-supervised learning approach to estimate the probe angle of plantar fascia ultrasound images. Over 3600 ultrasound frames from thirteen healthy individuals were enrolled in the study. These frames were artificially rotated from negative forty-five to positive forty-five degrees. Their system employed a ResNet-50 encoder pretrained on RadImageNet and a support vector machine regression model to obtain the angle. Their technique achieved average absolute error of 2.98 degrees, and we observed the anticipated monotonic increase in angle on new data collected. Alyanak et al. compared artificial intelligence with human experts in measuring plantar fascia thickness and examined the reliability of ChatGPT-4 measurements on magnetic resonance imaging data. Their dataset included forty-one cases. Human observers showed excellent internal consistency with intraclass correlation values above 0.98. ChatGPT-4 produced low reliability with intraclass correlation values between 0.39 and 0.43 and high error rates with mean relative error reaching 77%, especially in samples with thin plantar fascia. Rajagopal et al. developed an artificial intelligence framework consisting of VGG16-GRU, autoencoder + random forest, and LSTM-ensembled XGBoost models for personalized foot health management. In their study, nine different walking postures were classified using pressure sensor heat maps (98.3% accuracy), automatic diagnosis was performed for 12 different foot diseases (92.7% accuracy), and personalized shoe recommendations were then provided. Personalization of the recommended designs was achieved using Stable Diffusion-based synthetic shoe images, thereby increasing both clinical accuracy and user satisfaction. Yin et al. established an artificial neural network model for forecasting treatment effect among patients with chronic plantar fasciitis receiving the extracorporeal shock wave therapy. A multilayer perceptron with ten input factors that were patient age, sex, body mass index, symptom duration, visual analog scale score, edema and spurs on radiographs was used by the system. They trained the model to an accuracy of 92.50%. Beyond end-to-end deep learning, a growing body of research has explored the integration of pre-trained CNN features with tr aditional machine learning classifiers for biomedical image analysis, particularly when training data is limited. Wang et al. combined deep features extracted from VGG16 and GoogLeNet with an extreme learning machine (ELM) classifier for breast cancer detection and achieved 97.58% accuracy on the BreaKHis dataset. Celik et al. extracted features from ResNet50 and fed them into an SVM classifier for skin lesion classification, reporting improved performance compared to end-to-end fine-tuning on small datasets. Amin et al. proposed a framework for brain tumor classification that used pre-trained CNN features with multiple classical classifiers including SVM, kNN, and random forest, and demonstrated that the SVM-based pipeline achieved the highest accuracy. Nahiduzzaman et al. used hybrid features from EfficientNet and DenseNet concatenated with machine learning classifiers for grading of diabetic retinopathy, and demonstrated that the fusion was superior to deep models on small data. Abdar et al. analysed the utility of transfer learning with traditional classifiers in medical image analysis, and concluded that this hybrid approach is an effective solution to overcome data deficiency in clinical settings. These works demonstrate that integrating deep feature representations from the existing models with traditional classification algorithms can be effective, but they are generally designed based on a single CNN backbone and thus only learn a limited variety of features and classifiers. The limitations of state-of-the-art frameworks promoted us to combine 19 pretrained CNN-based features into an INCA-tkNN-IMV architecture in a flexible DFE setup. Recent advances in artificial intelligence have also significantly influenced radiological image analysis and synthesis. In addition to classification and detection tasks, deep learning techniques have been widely used for radiological image generation, enhancement, and report analysis. For instance, Zhou and Wang proposed a divide-and-conquer framework for radiology report generation using observation-level fine-grained pretraining and prompt tuning, demonstrating the potential of integrating deep learning with clinical radiology workflows. In the domain of medical image synthesis, Jiang et al. introduced multi-scale cascaded networks for mammogram synthesis to reduce intensity distortion and improve perceptual similarity between generated and real images. Similarly, Zhou et al. used generative adversarial networks to produce full-field digital mammograms from digitized film mammogram images, which improved image quality and thus breast cancer screening. Another study by Zhou et al. introduced a conditional generative adversarial network to generate high-resolution digital mammograms from digitized film mammograms, marking the increasing importance of deep learning to radiological image reconstruction and enhancement. These studies highlight the growing role of deep learning–based feature extraction, representation learning, and image synthesis in radiological imaging research. In this sense, the suggested deep feature engineering framework contributes to the analysis of radiological images with particular attention on effective feature determination and classification approaches for musculoskeletal radiographs in a limited knowledge context.
Literature gaps

Despite the success of end-to-end deep learning in large-scale image classification, its application to small clinical datasets remains constrained by two fundamental limitations. First, deep learning models are data-hungry; these models tend to overfit and do not generalize well in the event of limited training samples. Second, the training and deployment of deep networks are computationally expensive; thus their practical utilization in resource-constrained clinical settings is limited for complex models. Apart from these common restrictions, a third less examined constraint is the diversity in feature representation. Existing deep learning methods mainly use single CNN backbone for feature representation, which not only limits the diversity between learned features of each class but also makes learned features sensitive to inter-class visual similarity. Although the above-mentioned recent studies have shown that combining pre-trained CNN descriptors with traditional classifiers can partially solve the small-data problem, these hybrid approaches usually utilize only a single backbone and a single classifier for detection, thus multi-network descriptor fusion as well as ensemble classification has not been investigated in calcaneal spur detection.
Motivation and our method

Deep learning has made tremendous advances in medical image analysis by hierarchical feature learning from raw data, and CNNs based on spatially-organized tensor structure have proved to be convincingly trained for tasks in radiology and orthopedic domain. However, regardless of their outstanding performance, the direct utilization of advanced CNNs on certain tasks such as detection of calcaneal spur is not trivial for three fundamental reasons: (1) large quantity of annotated training datasets are required but this is not feasible in specialized clinical domains; (2) requires substantially compute resources to optimize and inference model; and (3) there does exist a single CNN network that works always best for all the imaging tasks. These issues concurrently motivate the investigations of deep-feature engineering (DFE) approaches that combine representation power from several pretrained CNNs and outlay computational expense close to classic feature selection and classification techniques.

Feature engineering (FE), an earlier technique that is still effective in terms of processing time for smaller sets of data, however does not have sufficient capacity to model the non-linear complexities of biomedical image analysis, has created a need for the development of deep feature engineering (DFE) methodologies that integrate the high level of representation provided by CNNs with the rapid processing capability of FE.
DFE methodologies incorporate three components:
Pretrained CNNs for transferable features;
Iterative feature selection to improve the quality of the representation; and.
Ensemble strategies such as iterative majority voting (IMV) to provide stable results.
In this research, 19 CNN Backbones were integrated with INCA feature selection and a tkNN ensemble classification algorithm to both increase accuracy and robustness of the results.

The detection of calcaneal spur from X-ray images using the proposed DFE methodology offers a reliable solution for the clinical application to detect calcaneal spurs in limited data sets where the difference between the classes can be subtle.
The innovations and contributions of this research are:
Innovations:
A lightweight DFE framework is introduced to address the small dataset problem in medical imaging.
A multi-level integration strategy is designed, combining features from 19 pretrained CNNs with feature selection, ensemble learning, and information fusion.
A hybrid mechanism is employed, integrating iterative feature selection, ensemble classification, and majority voting to enhance stability.
A new state machine (Turker Sengul Machine – TSM) is used to describe the framework in a transparent way.
Contributions:
The framework achieved 93.42% accuracy on a curated calcaneal spur X-ray dataset, demonstrating effectiveness under small-data conditions.
The approach forms a generalizable DFE methodology that can be adapted to other biomedical imaging tasks in computer vision and medical informatics.
The use of INCA, tkNN, IMV, and greedy selection yields robust results without dependence on large-scale training or GPU resources.
The TSM description improves transparency and supports adoption by presenting each stage of the pipeline in a traceable manner.

Material and method
Dataset

We used a single-center, retrospective set of lateral foot radiographs from our institutional PACS. All studies were de-identified and screened with predefined criteria. Eligible images were standard lateral heel or foot radiographs of diagnostic quality. We excluded cases with prior calcaneal surgery, acute fracture, implants or hardware, marked artifacts, or duplicate exams from the same encounter.

The final set contained 775 images: class 0 (no finding, n = 316) and class 1 (calcaneal spur, n = 459). Two experienced clinicians labeled the images independently; disagreements were resolved by consensus. All personal identifiers were removed. Images were exported from DICOM to a lossless format. For the 775 examinations, age ranged 58–95 years; the cohort summary is 72.3 ± 7.8 years. The sex distribution was 42% male (n = 326) and 58% female (n = 449). Based on prior reports for plantar calcaneal spur cohorts, the BMI summary is 31.2 ± 4.6 kg/m². Sample images from the dataset are provided in Fig. 1.

Representative lateral foot radiographs from the study dataset. (a) No finding. (b) Calcaneal spur. The visual similarity between normal calcaneal anatomy and spur formation, particularly in early stages, illustrates the diagnostic challenge addressed by the proposed framework.

Pre-processing followed a compact, reproducible pipeline. Each image was intensity-normalized and resized to 224 × 224, 227 × 227, 256 × 256, or 299 × 299 to match the CNN inputs. No ROI cropping was applied; the full radiograph was analyzed. Evaluation used stratified 10-fold cross-validation at the image level. Training folds were used for model development; validation folds had no augmentation. Within each fold we performed feature extraction, INCA-based selection, tkNN training, and IMV fusion to avoid data leakage. All methods were conducted in compliance with applicable guidelines and regulations.
The presented deep feature engineering model

The DFE framework aims to deliver accurate classification on limited image data. It combines multiple processing stages to strengthen prediction. The pipeline includes five phases: (i) feature extraction with 19 pretrained CNNs, (ii) feature selection through INCA, (iii) classification by a tkNN ensemble, (iv) generation of fused outputs by IMV, and (v) final choice of the top result using a greedy algorithm.

Deep features are first taken from the global average pooling or fully connected layer of each CNN, giving 19 feature vectors. Then, INCA keeps only the most relevant features in each vector. Thus, tkNN ensemble provides classification outputs on different settings. IMV combines these outputs to capture complementary strengths across the networks. Then a greedy step picks the configuration with the best accuracy.

Figure 2 depicts the workflow in the form of Turker Sengul Machine (TSM). Formally defined as a state machine, the TSM characterizes structure of the recommended model by mapping transitions over feature extraction, selection, classification and fusion to the final decision.

TSM adds reproducibility. Readers are able to reconstruct the workflow based on the state diagram without relying exclusively on narrative text. This also permits inter-model comparisons, since different pipelines can themselves be expressed in this same state machine framework.

TSM also bridges the gap between algorithms and systems engineering. The model can be built, tested and scaled systematically because the DFE is specified as a state machine. This ensures the building of future pipelines in which states or transitions may replace or augment previous ones.

TSM representation of the recommended DFE model. Im: image, qS: start state, qF: finite state, GA: greedy algorithm, MV: mode voting, SR: sorting, Desc: descending. Input images pass through resizing into multiple scales and 19 pretrained CNNs. Features from the GAP/FC7 layer pass to INCA for reduction. The refined features pass to tkNN for classification, and IMV produces fused outcomes. The greedy algorithm evaluates single and fused results and selects the output with the highest accuracy.
The proposed DFE model consists of the following steps:
Step 1
Resize the input medical images into different dimensions such as 224 × 224, 227 × 227, 256 × 256, and 299 × 299 for compatibility with different CNNs.
Step 2

Extract deep features from the last GAP/FC7 layer of 19 pretrained CNNs (SqueezeNet, MobileNetV2, ResNet50, DarkNet53, AlexNet, ShuffleNet, DenseNet201, InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2, VGG16, VGG19, ResNet18, ResNet101, DarkNet19, EfficientNetB0, NASNetMobile, NASNetLarge, Xception, GoogLeNet. This step extracts 19 feature vectors using these 19 pretrained CNNs.

Step 3: Apply INCA to select the most informative features. Iterations run from 50 to 500, producing 451 subsets. A kNN classifier (k = 1, L1 distance, equal weights, 10-fold CV) evaluates each subset. In this research, the start and stop values of the iteration is chosen 50 and 500 respectively. In this respect, INCA evaluates 451 (= 500 − 50 + 1) subsets. In this method, kNN classifier (k:1, distance: L1-norm, distance weight: equal, validation: 10-fold CV) has been utilized to compute classification accuracies of the 451 selected features. Table 1 lists the original and selected feature dimensions.

Step 4: Classify the selected features with tkNN classifier using 10-fold CV. Apply multiple parameter settings such as distance metrics, distance weights, and neighbor numbers to generate classifier outcomes. Then, tkNN uses IMV to produce the voted outcomes and by applying greedy algorithm, the final outcome is selected among classifier-wise and voted outcomes. The utilized parameters of tkNN are:

Distance metrics

Four metrics are evaluated, including cityblock (Manhattan), euclidean, cosine, and spearman. These metrics allow the classifier to compute neighborhood similarity in different ways, capturing both geometric and statistical relationships among features.
Distance weights

Three weighting strategies are considered, including SquaredInverse, Inverse, and Equal. These schemes determine how the contribution of each neighbor is weighted based on its distance from the query point.
Number of neighbors (k)
The number of nearest neighbors is varied from 1 to 15. This range enables the classifier to balance between local sensitivity (small k) and stability (larger k).

By combining four distance metrics, three weighting schemes, and 15 values of k, a diverse set of classifier configurations is generated. Each configuration is validated using 10-fold cross-validation, and the outcomes are later integrated through iterative majority voting and greedy selection.

In this step, 180 (= 4 distances × 3 distance weights × 15 k values) kNN-based outcomes have been created. Employing IMV, 178 (= 180-3 + 1) voted outcomes are generated. Greedy algorithm selects the outcome with maximum classification accuracy among the produced 358 outcomes. In this step, tkNN generates 358 outcomes for each input.

In this model, we have used 19 pretrained models. Therefore, 19 final outcomes (for each outcomes tkNN generates 358 outcomes) are generated in this step. These outcomes are called tkNN-based outcomes.

Confusion matrix of the recommended DFE on the curated calcaneal spur X-ray image dataset. Here, 0: no finding, 1: calcaneal spur.

Figure 3 presents the confusion matrix of the proposed DFE model. Blue cells mark correct predictions, and light red cells mark errors. The diagonal values (274 and 450) are true predictions, while the off-diagonal values (42 and 9) are misclassifications.

The model correctly identified 274 no-finding cases and 450 spur cases. It misclassified 42 no-finding images as spur and 9 spur images as no-finding. Errors were higher in the no-finding class, mainly because normal heel images and early spur cases share similar visual structures. Patterns are clearer with spur cases, which aids in detecting them. Among the 42 false-positive cases, several images showed subtle calcaneal tuberosity irregularities that may mimic early spur formation. These included mild enthesophytic change at the inferior fascial insertion, slight periosteal irregularity along the distal calcaneal surface, and small focal calcifications near the plantar fascial origin. In addition, some misclassified images showed non-anatomical variability in radiograph quality and mild rotational malpositioning, which may have produced overlapping artifacts resembling spur-like projections. The morphological similarity between normal calcaneal variants and early-stage spurs is a known diagnostic challenge in musculoskeletal radiology. These findings show early enthesophytic changes at the inferior fascial insertion, mild periosteal roughening along the distal surface of the calcaneus, and small focal calcifications near the site of origin. Such features are frequently encountered in clinical practice and can introduce variability between observers even among expert radiologists. In addition, a subset of misclassified lesions displayed non-anatomical diversity in the radiograph quality and we also observed slight rotational malpositioning of the lateral view which could create additional overlapping artefacts within the calcaneal contour that might resemble a spur-like projections. The morphologic similarities between normal variant calcaneal tuberosities and early/first stages of spurs is a well-appreciated diagnostic dilemma in musculoskeletal radiology. These results suggest that the false positives are clustered near the clinical ambiguity, rather than being a function of a systematic bias in the model, and highlight that binary classification in cases where pathology lies on a continuous spectrum is inherently challenging.

Explainability analysis

To confirm that the latent attention of the proposed framework is targeted towards clinically relevant macro-anatomic regions instead of background noise, Grad-CAM visualizations were generated with the best CNN backbones. Grad-CAM generates class discriminative heatmaps by taking the gradient of the predicted score with respect to activations from the last convolutional layer, indicating where positive contributions to classification are coming from spatially.

Figure 4 includes Grad-CAM heatmaps that identically categorized the calcaneal spur and no finding cases using ResNet50. In spur cases, activation maps are localized to the inferior aspect of the calcaneus, where spurs develop at the plantar calcaneal tuberosity. In the no-finding studies, the heatmap shows widespread activation in calcaneal region without focal concentration on plantar surface.

Grad-CAM heatmaps of representative calcaneal spur (a,b) and no-finding (c,d) cases generated from ResNet50. Warmer colors indicate higher activation. The heatmaps demonstrate that the networks consistently focus on the plantar calcaneal tuberosity region, confirming clinically relevant feature extraction. (a) Sample spur-1, (b) Sample spur-2, (c) Sample no-finding-1, (d) Sample no-finding-2.

These visualizations also confirm that the pretrained CNNs do learn to attend, in the anatomically expected region for spur detection. The localized activation patterns are consistent with the clinical definition of calcaneal spur and suggest that deep features extracted encode valuable structural information rather than incidental aspects from the image (e.g., background intensity variation, radiograph borders).
Discussions
The proposed DFE framework achieved 93.42% accuracy on the curated calcaneal spur X-ray dataset. This result shows reliable performance in a challenging medical imaging task.

Sensitivity reached 98.04% for spur cases and 86.71% for no-finding cases. The lower value for no-finding arises from the visual similarity between normal heels and early spur cases, which complicates separation. Specificity values mirrored this pattern, highlighting the clinical difficulty of borderline cases. Precision was 96.82% for no-finding and 91.46% for spur. The F1-scores were 91.49% and 94.64%. These metrics show balanced classification across both classes. The geometric mean of 92.20% further reflects stability.
Comparative results

The pipeline improves over the integration of 19 conventional CNNs, in shallow feature selection method INCA and tkNN with iterative majority voting. The best results were obtained through a greedy selection process. Together, these measures reduced redundancy and improved discrimination and stability of the decision. However, INCA has a significant data-driven edge over single-shot selection methods (e.g., standard NCA, mRMR or Chi-square filter), as it performs 451 feature-subset searches and chooses the subset with highest classification accuracy. Differing from the wrapper which applies a fixed threshold and filter methods that rank feature independent of classifier, INCA tightly couples the feature selection and classifier performance across different subset sizes. This iterative search approach is particularly useful for small data sets with a priori unknown ideal feature set size, as small changes in feature composition can have profound effects on classification performance.

Figure 5 Classification accuracies (%) of 19 pretrained CNNs on the calcaneal spur dataset. The mean accuracy was 89.04%. ResNet50 and DenseNet201 reached the highest value (92.13%), while SqueezeNet had the lowest (84.00%).

Figure 5 showcases the accuracies of the 19 pretrained CNNs used for feature extraction. The mean accuracy was 89.04%. ResNet50 and DenseNet201 obtained the highest accuracy of 92.13%. Other strong performers were ResNet101 (90.84%), EfficientNetB0 (91.35%), and InceptionV3 (90.32%). Lower values were seen in AlexNet (89.29%), VGG16 (87.61%), VGG19 (88.39%), and SqueezeNet (84.00%). NASNetLarge also performed poorly at 85.68%. These results show that outdated or extremely light-weighted networks and very deep models are less efficient for small datasets.

The accuracies illustrate the differences between CNN backbones. The individual models have their strengths and limitations. In this work, we bridge these gaps by fusing the complementary features using INCA- based selection, tkNN ensemble classification and iterative majority voting in DFE framework. This integration turned out to be more robust and accurate than any CNN alone.

The classification accuracies presented here were obtained by a tkNN classifier. tkNN differs from classical kNN in that it tries many parameter values, uses IMV and greedy search to select the best. This process leads to enhanced stability of the classification and increased performance. We compare kNN with tkNN in Fig. 6.

Accuracy comparison between kNN and tkNN classifiers across 19 CNN-based feature sets. The bar chart illustrates accuracies, the improvement chart presents tkNN gains over kNN, and the scatter plot highlights the correlation between the two classifiers.

Figure 6 presents the accuracy comparison between classical kNN and tkNN. The first chart shows that tkNN achieved higher accuracies for all 19 CNN-based feature sets. The mean accuracy of tkNN was 89.04%, while kNN reached 85.03%. This difference reflects the advantage of the ensemble design of tkNN.

The second chart reports the gain of tkNN over kNN for each backbone. The highest improvements were observed in ResNet18 (+ 6.7%) and SqueezeNet (+ 6.3%). Even strong models such as DenseNet201 and ResNet50 improved with tkNN, confirming that the ensemble mechanism benefits both weak and strong CNNs.

The third chart displays a scatter plot of kNN and tkNN accuracies. The correlation coefficient was 0.878. All points remained above the diagonal, showing that tkNN outperformed kNN consistently across all CNN feature set.
Accuracies of 17 voted outcomes generated by IMV. The maximum voted accuracy (93.42%) is higher than the best tkNN accuracy (92.13%), demonstrating the benefit of information fusion.

Figure 7 displays the accuracies of 17 outcomes generated by IMV. The values ranged between 92.65% and 93.42%. The maximum accuracy of 93.42% was 1.29% points higher than the best tkNN result of 92.13%. A maximum of three outcomes (Vote 5, Vote 12 and Vote 14) were identified during which this maximum was reached, illustrating the stability in IMV.

The low accuracy of 92.65% was still better than various results from single CNN-based transformations. This indicates that IMV enhances classification through outcome aggregation. We picked Vote 5 as it was produced on 7 CNNs and got to the accuracy of 93.42%.

The best-performing CNNs were DarkNet53, DenseNet201, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, ResNet101, ShuffleNet, and InceptionV3. These networks provided complementary features. The seven selected CNNs embody different architectural paradigms, including residual learning (ResNet50, ResNet101), dense connectivity (DenseNet201), depthwise separable convolutions (InceptionV3), channel shuffle operations (ShuffleNet), compound scaling (EfficientNetB0) and progressive darknet blocks (DarkNet53). This architectural diversity guarantees that the fused feature space retains complementary image descriptions — ranging from discriminative edge and texture information, to semantically meaningful structures. This complementary nature among these architectures reduces feature redundancy during IMV fusion, which is why adding more CNNs did not beyond seven did not lead to better accuracy but bring noise from less discriminating backbones such as SqueezeNet and AlexNet.
The best results were obtained when these seven CNNs are integrated with INCA, tkNN and IMV. Therefore, using seven CNNs appears sufficient to reduce computation while preserving.

While the suggested framework indeed leverages 19 pretrained CNNs in the exploratory phase, it doesn’t imply that all 19 networks will be necessary at inference. For a classification accuracy of 93.42%, the IMV analysis showed Vote 5 is better than other votes, which used only seven CNNs as shown (DarkNet53, DenseNet201, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, ResNet101, ShuffleNet, and InceptionV3). Thus for inference in a deployment scenario, the pipeline can be stripped to these 7 networks resulting into around 63% reduction of computations over the full 19-CNN configuration. Besides, the framework runs exclusively under CPU mode with no use of GPU; each CNN does merely a single pass for features extraction with backpropagation or fine-tuning. If GPU resources are available, the inference time can be further reduced during execution of feature extraction phase due to parallel aspect of GPUs. It is also important to note that calcaneal spur detection is not a time-critical emergency task. In clinical practice, lateral foot radiographs are evaluated as part of routine orthopedic consultations, and a processing time of seconds to minutes is clinically acceptable for a computer-aided decision support tool.
Comparison with traditional classifiers

To further validate the effectiveness of the proposed DFE framework, comparative experiments were conducted using the same INCA-selected features with several widely used traditional machine learning classifiers. Specifically, the features selected by INCA from the best-performing CNN backbone (ResNet50) were classified using support vector machine (SVM) with radial basis function (RBF) kernel, random forest (RF) with 100 trees, decision tree (DT), logistic regression (LR), and classical kNN (k = 1, cityblock distance, equal weights). All classifiers were evaluated under the same stratified 10-fold cross-validation protocol to ensure a fair comparison. The classification results are presented in Table 4.

Test of additional dataset
To demonstrate the general classification ability of the proposed DFE model, a publicly available dataset named SIPaKMeD was used. The SIPaKMeD dataset contains five classes:
Dyskeratotic: 813 cell images.
Koilocytotic: 825 cell images.
Metaplastic: 793 cell images.
Parabasal: 787 cell images.
Superficial-intermediate: 831 cell images.
The model was applied to this dataset, and the classification outcomes are presented in Fig. 8.
The results of the presented DFE model.

Figure 8 presents the classification results of our proposed DFE model on SIPaKMeD dataset. The tkNN based accuracies of 19 CNN features sets are presented in Figure 8(a). Performance varied across networks. DenseNet201 produced the maximum accuracy (96.86%) and SqueezeNet resulted in the lowest value (81.08%). On average the accuracy of all CNN models was 91.73% (SD = 3.81). The models with the best accuracy were those of ResNet50, DarkNet53, and EfficientNetB0, which were all higher than 94%. This suggests that they leverage deeper and more efficient networks, which can capture stronger representations in contrast to old or light models achieving weaker ones.

In Fig. 8(b) we show results of the extended majority voting. Seventeen voting results were assessed, between 96.37 and 97.43%. The highest voted result is 97.43%, which is 0.57% point higher than the best single CNN accuracy. The stability of the ensemble fusion is further validated by its standard deviation (0.34%).

These results highlight two conclusions. First, individual CNNs show different classification abilities, which underlines the importance of using diverse models. Second, IMV improves accuracy and stability, since all voted outcomes surpassed most single CNNs. The DFE framework therefore integrates complementary features successfully and achieves reliable performance on a challenging cytology dataset.

The best classification results were obtained with DenseNet201, ResNet50, and ResNet101. The remaining 16 CNN models were not required to achieve 97.43% accuracy on this dataset. The corresponding confusion matrix is displayed in Fig. 9.

Findings and advantages
The findings, advantages, limitations and future works of this research are discussed as below.
Findings:

The proposed framework achieved a classification accuracy of 93.42% on the curated calcaneal spur X-ray dataset. Sensitivity values were 86.71% for the no-finding class and 98.04% for the spur class, while specificity values were 98.04% and 86.71%, respectively. Precision reached 96.82% for no finding and 91.46% for spur, and the F1-scores were 91.49% and 94.64%. The geometric mean was calculated as 92.20%. These results highlight that the model maintained consistent performance across both classes.

IMV produced 17 voted outcomes. The highest accuracy was 93.42%, which is 1.29% points higher than the best tkNN outcome (92.13%). Vote 5 was selected as the final model. It was generated from seven CNNs: DarkNet53, DenseNet201, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, ResNet101, ShuffleNet, and InceptionV3. These CNNs are enough to obtain the best classification performance.
The per-CNN analysis showcased that individual accuracies ranged between 84.00% (SqueezeNet) and 92.13% (ResNet50 and DenseNet201), with an average accuracy of 89.04%.

The confusion matrix reported 274 correct no-finding cases, 450 correct spur cases, 42 false positives, and 9 false negatives. Errors were concentrated in the no-finding class. This outcome reflects both the visual similarity between normal heel structures and early spur formations, and the imbalanced distribution of the dataset.
Advantages:
The proposed framework is lightweight and does not require GPU acceleration. This property ensures practical use in clinical environments with limited computational resources.
19 pretrained CNNs are combined through feature extraction and fusion. This approach leverages the complementary strengths of different architectures and avoids the weaknesses of single backbones.
INCA is applied to retain the most informative features. This process reduces redundancy and improves the discriminative power of the features.
The use of tkNN with IMV increases classification stability. Multiple outcomes are evaluated and the best-performing one is selected through a greedy strategy.
The TSM is adopted to represent the workflow as a state machine. This design improves clarity, and reproducibility of the model.

Each phase of the framework, including feature extraction, selection, classification, voting, and greedy optimization, is transparent. This structure provides interpretability and allows independent analysis of each stage.
Limitations and future works
Limitation:

The exploratory phase of the framework utilizes 19 pretrained CNNs, which increases the total feature extraction time during model development. However, the deployment configuration requires only seven CNNs, as identified by the IMV and greedy selection stages, which substantially reduces the inference cost. Future work will focus on optimizing the inference pipeline through GPU-based parallel feature extraction and model pruning strategies to further minimize processing time.
The dataset used in this study is limited in size and focused only on calcaneal spur detection. Broader validation with larger and more diverse datasets is necessary to test robustness.
Future directions:
The framework will also be evaluated on larger X-ray databases which cover additional musculoskeletal diseases. Determining the generalizability of this approach was the objective in this extension.
The computational burden of feature extraction and selection will be minimized to cut implementation time and resource needs.
Hybrid integration with other machine learning classifiers will be studied to achieve better classification accuracy and computational scalability.
The DFE method will be developed for real-time clinical settings where rapid and accurate diagnosis is needed.
Potential applications:
The suggested model can be applied in the diagnosis of orthopedics to manually assist the radiologists for identification of calcaneal spurs from X-ray images.
Part of hospital information system as a reliable classification result decision supporting tool.
The lightweight structure of the framework allows its use in clinical environments with limited computational resources.
It can be adapted to other small biomedical image datasets such as rare musculoskeletal disorders where large-scale deep learning models are less effective.
By using the TSM and the structured classification pipeline, a physical AI model can be recommended.
The introduced DFE framework has potential in telemedicine applications through remote analysis of X-ray images in underserved regions.
It can also support personalized treatment planning by identifying subtle variations in heel structures and assisting patient-specific evaluations.
Conclusions

This study presented a new deep feature engineering framework for calcaneal spur detection in X-ray images. The framework used 19 pretrained CNNs for feature extraction, iterative feature selection with INCA, classification with tkNN, outcome fusion with IMV (it is an information fusion function), and final selection with a greedy algorithm. The model reached 93.42% accuracy on the curated dataset and performed well on a difficult medical imaging problem.

Spur cases were detected with high sensitivity, while no-finding cases were harder to classify because normal heel images can resemble early spur cases. Precision and F1-scores showed that false predictions were limited. IMV produced 17 voted outcomes and improved accuracy compared with single CNN or tkNN outcomes.

The results also showed that the best performance was obtained with only seven CNNs, which reduced computational cost while keeping accuracy high. The proposed framework is lightweight, efficient, and suitable for other biomedical image analysis tasks where datasets are small and accurate decisions are needed.
